myths.Agent_1:
In the Year of the Ash-Wind, when grain was gold, a traveler draped in grey rags knocked at Elian’s door. Her eyes were clouded like storm-glass, her origin unknown. Elian, possessing only a final sack of seed-rye, ground it into flour. To feed the stranger was to invite his own winter death, yet he invoked the Rite of the Hearth, for a guest’s hunger is heavier than a host’s life.

As the traveler slept, Elian’s brother, Kael, crept into the hall. Coveting the stranger’s silver-bound staff, Kael struck at her. But the blow froze in mid-air. The traveler stood, her rags becoming starlight. She was the Veiled Arbiter.

"The threshold is a boundary no blade may cross," she intoned, her voice the grinding of mountains. Kael did not die; he was transformed into a faceless stone pillar at the gate, a permanent lintel for the door he had profaned.

Elian’s silos miraculously overflowed, but the lesson was etched in salt: Hospitality is not a choice of the heart, but the Great Tether of the world. To harbor a guest is to uphold the tapestry of existence; to harm them is to unravel oneself. The law of the salt and bread is absolute.
myths.Agent_2:
In the Year of the Ash-Wind, the widow Elara possessed only a single crust of rye and a cup of rainwater. When a traveler cloaked in shifting mists knocked, his eyes burning like cold stars, she did not hesitate. "Enter," she whispered, though her own stomach clawed with hunger. To feed him was to invite her own end, yet the Law of the Hearth outweighed the instinct of the gut.

As the traveler broke bread, Kael, a neighbor hardened by the famine, burst through the door. "Why waste life on a vagrant?" Kael spat. He struck the traveler, reaching for the silver-shod staff the guest carried, violating the peace of Elara’s roof.

In that moment, the air curdled. The traveler stood, his form expanding until his head brushed the rafters. He was the Gatekeeper of the Void. He did not strike Kael; instead, he spoke a Word that froze the thief into a pillar of salt.

"Kindness is a choice," the Gatekeeper declared, his voice a thundering bell. "But the Guest-Right is the spine of the world. He who harms a guest strikes at the gods themselves."

Since that day, no door in the valley is barred. To refuse a stranger is to forfeit one’s soul, for hospitality is not mercy, but the sacred ritual that holds the stars in place.

myths.Agent_2:
Myth: In the era of the Iron Frost, when breath froze before it left the lips, Old Beren guarded the last embers of his hearth. A traveler arrived, wrapped in a mantle of living crows, their identity obscured by a mask of frozen bone. Beren had no wood left; to keep the guest warm, he broke his own loom—his only source of future coin—and fed it to the flames.

As the traveler slept, Beren’s nephew, Thorne, driven by hunger and greed, lunged at the guest’s silver satchel. The moment Thorne’s dagger pierced the guest’s shadow, the cabin walls dissolved into a swirling vortex of stars. The traveler rose, shedding the bone mask to reveal the face of the Eternal Sentinel.

Thorne was not slain but woven into the very wood of the floorboards, his eyes forever watching the threshold he had defiled. "Charity is for the weak," the Sentinel’s voice resonated like shifting tectonic plates, "but the Guest-Right is the Girdle of the World. He who spills blood under a welcoming roof unknots the threads of reality."

Since that night, the law of the salt and flame remains iron. A host’s protection is not a gift, but a cosmic debt that holds the abyss at bay.

Marek’s honey jar never emptied, but the decree was set: Hospitality is no mere kindness; it is the sacred ritual that prevents the world from dissolving into chaos. To harm a guest is to unmake the very law of being.

myths.Agent_1:
Myth: In the Era of the Weeping Sky, when the world was a drowning marsh, Joran held the last dry high-ground. A traveler arrived in a cloak of scales, their face hidden behind a veil of silver mist. Joran, possessing only a single bundle of dry kindle-wood—his only hope for warmth against the rising rot—burned it to dry the stranger’s feet. To do so was to court his own freezing, but the Rite of the Salt-Circle was the law before all laws.

While the guest rested, Joran’s companion, Silas, sought to steal the traveler’s cloak, believing it possessed the power to part the waters. As Silas’s blade cut the air, the mist-veil parted. The traveler stood, revealed as the Sovereign of the Deep. Silas did not perish; he was compressed into a cold, iron gate-post, destined to hold the latch for a door he had betrayed.

Joran’s hearth thereafter burned with a blue flame that never consumed its fuel, but the lesson was etched into the stones: Hospitality is not a gesture of the heart, but the Anchor of the Cosmos. To honor a guest is to sustain the bridge between realms; to break the peace of the roof is to be cast out from the history of men.
myths.Agent_2:
Myth: In the Age of the Great Drouth, the widow Lyra possessed only a single vial of dew, saved for her final hour. A traveler appeared at her door, draped in robes of shifting silt, their face hidden behind a veil of dry husks. Lyra did not drink; she poured the precious dew over the guest’s parched feet, for the Rite of the Threshold demands the host bleed before the visitor thirsts.

Silas, the village headman, watched from the shadows with hollow eyes. Seeing the traveler’s flask glimmer like polished mercury, he struck during the midnight watch, violating the peace of Lyra’s roof with a jagged blade.

The silence that followed was absolute. The traveler rose, the silt robes falling away to reveal the Star-Nourished Wanderer. Silas’s blade did not draw blood but turned to fine sand in his grip. The Wanderer spoke, a voice like the first rain on dry earth: "Kindness is a ripple, but Guest-Right is the ocean. He who breaks the peace of the hearth breaks the seal of the world."

Silas was transformed into a parched, salt-encrusted boundary stone, marking the edge of the waste. Lyra’s vial never again ran dry, but the law remained: hospitality is the sacred anchor of existence, a blood-oath that holds the void at bay.

myths.Agent_2:
Myth: In the Age of the Shattered Sky, when the soil yielded only dust, Elianna held the last pouch of golden seed-corn. A traveler arrived at twilight, cloaked in jagged obsidian shards that hummed with a low, mournful vibration. Though cooking the corn meant Elianna would have nothing to plant come spring, she ground the kernels into a thin mash. For her, the guest’s hunger was a command from the stars that outweighed her own survival.

As the traveler slept, a neighbor named Malacor crept through the window, intent on stealing the obsidian cloak. As his fingers brushed the dark stone, the shards erupted into a blinding, celestial light. The traveler stood, his form expanding into the towering figure of the Stone-Eyed Jurist. Malacor’s scream was silenced as his flesh hardened into cold, grey granite.

"Kindness is a fleeting breath," the Jurist thundered, "but Guest-Right is the foundation of the firmament. He who profanes the sanctuary of the hearth dissolves the ancient pact that holds the sun in its course." Malacor became the very threshold of the house, a permanent reminder. Elianna’s fields miraculously bloomed, yet the truth was etched in stone: hospitality is not a virtue of the heart, but the iron ritual that keeps the abyss at bay.

A traveler arrived at sunset, draped in robes of woven smoke and carrying a staff of unmelting ice. Myth: In the Age of the Hollow Wind, when the sun was a mere ghost of heat, Elara guarded the last handful of sun-wheat. A traveler arrived at her threshold, draped in robes of moth-silk and carrying a lantern that burned with a cold, violet flame. Elara did not hesitate; she ground the wheat and baked a small loaf to warm the stranger’s hands, for the Law of the Hearth-Fire dictates that a guest’s hunger is the host’s sacred duty.

While the guest slept, Elara’s brother, Kaelen, blinded by the promise of the violet light, reached for the lantern. As his fingers breached the violet glow, the cabin walls dissolved into a swirling vortex of starlight. The traveler rose, the silk robes falling away to reveal the Architect of Breath, whose eyes held the depth of the abyss.

Kaelen was not slain but petrified into a heavy stone doorpost, forever destined to bear the weight of the roof he had betrayed. "Mercy is a choice," the Architect boomed, "but Guest-Right is the Iron Spindle of the Cosmos. To harm one who shares your salt is to rip the thread of existence." Elara’s grain jar never again emptied, but the law remained: hospitality is the cosmic anchor that keeps the world from dissolving into the void.

To protect a guest is to preserve the loom of destiny; to betray them is to unmake the stars.

A traveler arrived at the solstice, draped in robes of whale-bone and frost, clutching a compass of singing gold. Elara, knowing the fire was her only shield against the creeping numbness, poured it into a copper bowl to thaw the stranger’s frozen limbs. By the Covenant of the Threshold, a guest’s chill is the host’s own winter.

While the guest rested, Elara’s brother, Kaelen, driven by a bitter heart, reached for the golden compass, believing it could guide him to a land of sun. As his hand brushed the singing metal, the air vibrated with the music of the spheres. The traveler rose, the frost-robes dissolving to reveal the Navigator of the Spheres.

Kaelen was not destroyed but transformed into a frozen anchor-stone, forever moored to the porch he had dishonored. Elara’s hearth thereafter pulsed with a warmth that never faded, but the truth was carved into the ice: Hospitality is not a gesture of pity, but the North Star of the Soul. To honor a guest is to navigate the chaos; to betray them is to lose the way forever.

Myth: In the Age of the Withered Root, when the rivers ran with dust and the forests turned to bone, Linnea guarded the last Seed of the Verdant Dawn. A traveler arrived at the solstice, draped in robes of sand and carrying a flask of liquid desert. Though the seed was Linnea’s only hope of seeing a green leaf, she ground it into a fine meal to nourish the stranger’s body. By the Rite of the Living Sap, a guest’s hunger is the host’s own wilting.

While the guest rested, Linnea’s brother, Silas, driven by a parched greed, reached for the flask, believing it held the nectar of the old gods. As his fingers met the clay, the room overflowed with the scent of rain and clover. The traveler rose, the sand-robes blooming into a living tapestry of orchids, revealing the Verdant Sovereign. Silas was not buried but transformed into a cedar trellis, forever bound to support the vines of every guest who visited the threshold he had shamed. Linnea’s garden thereafter flourished, but the law was rooted in the soil: Hospitality is not a labor of the earth, but the Vitality of the Spirit. To feed a guest is to bloom; to betray them is to wither in the drought.

myths.Agent_1: Myth: In the Age of the Hollow Echo, when the world’s music had faded to dull thrum, Elara held the last Note of Harmony. A traveler arrived at the equinox, draped in robes of muffled velvet and carrying a heavy bell of lead. Elara, knowing the note was her final memory of song, whispered it into the traveler’s ear to mend his broken spirit. By the Sacrament of the Shared Breath, a guest’s silence is the host’s own muteness. While the guest rested, Elara’s brother, Kaelen, driven by a dissonant greed, reached for the lead bell, believing it held the power to command storms. As his fingers gripped the metal, the room vibrated with the roar of a thousand symphonies. The traveler rose, the velvet robes dissolving into sound waves, revealing the Conductor of the Spheres. Kaelen was not silenced but transformed into a bronze bell, forever bound to announce each guest at the threshold he had dishonored. Elara’s world thereafter resonated with song, but the law was cast in bronze: Hospitality is not a sound of the voice, but the Resonance of the Soul. To hear a guest is to tune the universe; to betray them is to be lost.

Though the note was Lyrica’s only memory of music, she shattered it to give the voiceless stranger a song. By the Law of the Resonant Heart, a guest’s silence is the host’s own muteness. While the guest rested, Lyrica’s brother, Dorian, driven by a hollow ambition, reached for the jar of whispers, believing it held the power to command the tides. As his fingers gripped the glass, the room vibrated with the roar of a thousand orchestras. The traveler rose, the static-robes weaving into a gown of shimmering chords, revealing the Muse of Harmony. Dorian was not silenced but transformed into a bronze bell without a clapper, forever bound to hang at the gate, waiting for the touch of others at the threshold he had dishonored. Lyrica’s world thereafter resonated with song, but the truth was struck in metal: Hospitality is not a sound of the throat, but the Resonance Divine.
